title: Krupnik
subtitle: Polish barley and vegetable soup
excerpt: Pearl barley simmered in a rich chicken broth with root vegetables, dried porcini, and dill. The most comforting of the Polish everyday soups — thick, earthy, and made better the next day.
sourceNotes: Krupnik (from the Polish word krupa, meaning groats or grain) is one of the oldest soups in Polish cookery, mentioned in historical records from the sixteenth century. Pearl barley and root vegetables cooked in bone broth formed part of the everyday diet across Poland, Belarus, and Ukraine. The addition of dried mushrooms is a Polish feature found in many regional versions.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze without the fresh dill. The barley will absorb more liquid on freezing; thin with a little stock when reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Add extra stock when reheating as the barley continues to absorb liquid in the fridge.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better on day two. Make up to 3 days ahead and refrigerate.

content.text: Krupnik is a thick barley soup that sits between a broth and a stew. The pearl barley swells as it cooks and releases starch into the liquid, giving the finished soup a body that is impossible to achieve with any other grain. It is the soup that Polish families make when someone needs feeding up, when the weather turns, or when there is a leftover chicken carcass in the refrigerator.

Dried porcini add a depth that lifts the broth considerably. Soak them for 30 minutes, then add both the mushrooms and the strained soaking liquid to the pot. They are not essential, but the soup is noticeably better with them.

items.prepNote: soaked in 200 ml warm water for 30 minutes

content.text: A stockpot or large saucepan with a lid. The barley needs room to swell.

If using dried porcini, lift them from their soaking water and roughly chop; strain the soaking liquid through a fine sieve. Bring the {{stock-chicken}} to a simmer in a large pot. Add the {{pearl-barley}}, {{onion}}, {{carrot}}, {{parsnip}}, {{celeriac}}, {{leek}}, {{bay-leaves}}, and {{allspice}}. If using porcini, add them now along with their strained soaking liquid.

Simmer partially covered for 45 to 55 minutes, until the barley is completely tender and has thickened the broth. The soup should be thick. If it seems thin, simmer uncovered for another 10 minutes. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Remove the bay leaves and allspice berries.
Stir in the {{unsalted-butter}} if using, then ladle into bowls and scatter with {{dill}}. Serve with bread.

items.symptom: Barley still chalky in the centre
items.fix: Continue simmering until the barley is completely tender with no chalky core. Older barley can take up to 70 minutes

items.cause: Too little stock or cooked uncovered too long
items.fix: Add more hot stock or water, a ladleful at a time, and stir to incorporate. Adjust seasoning after thinning

items.cause: Stock too light or no dried mushrooms
items.fix: Add a small splash of cider vinegar to lift the soup. Dried porcini make a substantial difference to the broth depth

content.text: Krupnik appears in nearly every Polish regional tradition. It was long associated with peasant cooking: bone broth, whatever roots were available, and barley from the store. In modern Polish home cooking, it remains a default soup for illness, cold weather, and feeding children. The dill added at the end is non-negotiable in Polish households. The soup improves markedly on the second day, once the barley has had time to absorb the broth and the flavours have deepened.
